claims.text: According to Alex Hormozi, every business that customers perceive as excellent has clear standards, and every business customers perceive as poor lacks them — and the presence or absence of standards is entirely the owner's responsibility, not the team's.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the vast majority of business owners fail to translate standards into observable, actionable behaviors that their teams can replicate — the standard exists in the owner's head but is never operationalized into something the team can measure or follow.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that an owner who demands a behavior (such as punctuality) but does not personally model it loses all authority to enforce that standard — teams only take behavioral expectations seriously when the owner embodies them first.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: owner sets a behavioral standard for the team
owner does not personally exhibit the standard
claims.mechanism: Teams calibrate their behavior to what the owner does, not what the owner says; a gap between owner instruction and owner behavior signals that the standard is not truly held, removing the enforcement credibility.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi claims that for a leader to credibly ask others to change a behavior, the leader must first demonstrate that they have made that change themselves — requesting behavioral change from a position of non-compliance produces zero effect.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: leader is requesting a behavioral change from a team member or subordinate
claims.mechanism: Credibility for behavioral instruction derives from demonstrated personal compliance; without it, the request is perceived as do-as-I-say rather than do-as-I-do, and compliance is not achieved.

examples.text: Alex Hormozi recounts the Gandhi story as an illustration of leading by example before giving instruction: a mother asks Gandhi to tell her child to stop eating sugar. Gandhi asks her to return in a month. When she returns, Gandhi simply tells the child to stop eating sugar, and the child complies. When the mother asks why it took a month, Gandhi replies that he had to stop eating sugar himself first. Hormozi uses this story to argue that a leader cannot credibly demand a behavior they do not yet practice.

practices.text: Translate every standard you hold for your team into a specific, observable action — not a value or feeling, but a behavior you can see and measure.
Before requiring any behavior of your team, audit whether you personally model that behavior; if you do not, make the change in yourself first.
